coredata.dc:title: Corrosion behavior and biological activity of micro-arc oxidation coating with puerarin on pure magnesium surface
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Shuhao
Li, Muqin
Wang, Jingyan
Tian, Qinwen
Wang, Songge
Tang, Biao
coredata.dc:description: In order to improve the corrosion resistance and biological activity of micro-arc oxidation (MAO) coating on Mg alloys, a puerarin-loaded (PL) coating was prepared and applied on the MAO coating by the method of dip-coating in phytic acid (PA) solution with puerarin. The chemical and morphological characterizations of the coatings were examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). The corrosion properties of the coatings were studied by potentiodynamic polarization and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy in simulated body fluid (SBF). Besides, the bioactivity properties of the coatings in SBF were investigated following 3, 7, and 14 days of immersion. The results indicate that the defects of through-pores weaken the corrosion resistance of the MAO and PL coatings with a high cross-link density sealing of the pores of the MAO coating. The higher initial impedance, lower current density, and loss of weight show that PL coatings have higher corrosion resistance than do MAO coatings. Furthermore, PL coatings improve the bioactivity of the MAO coating by accelerating apatite mineralization. The use of such a coating is expected to promote the application of Mg in bone repair materials and improve bone healing rate in the early stages of implantation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Micro-arc oxidation
Magnesium
Biodegradable
Corrosion
Bone repair
Puerarin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Surface morphologies and corresponding EDS spectra of coatings (a) MAO; (b) PA; (c) PL-0.01 g/mL; (d) PL-0.02 g/mL; and (e) PL-0.03 g/mL.
Cross-sections and corresponding elemental distribution maps of coatings (a) MAO; (b) PA; and (c) PL-0.02 g/mL.

(a) XPS survey spectrum of different coatings; (b, c, d) XPS detailed Mg 1s, O 1s and Si 2p regions of the MAO coating; (e, f, g) XPS detailed C 1s, O 1s and P 2p regions of the PA coating; and (h, i) XPS detailed C 1s and O 1s regions of the PL-0.02 g/mL coating.
FTIR spectra of coatings (a) MAO; (b) PA; and (c) PL-0.02 g/mL.
Potentiodynamic polarization curves of the MAO, PA, and PL coatings.
Nyquist and Bode diagrams and equivalent circuits of different coatings (a) Nyquist plots; (b, c) Bode plots; and (d) equivalent circuits.
SEM micrographs after immersion in SBF of (a, d, g) MAO coatings; (b, e, h) PA coatings; and (c, f, i) PL-0.02 g/mL coatings.
Rates of weight loss of MAO, PA, and PL-0.02 g/mL coatings.
XRD patterns of MAO, PA, and PL-0.02 g/mL coatings after immersion in SBF for 7 days.
The atomic contents for different coatings from XPS study.
Results of corrosion current densities and corrosion potentials for different coatings in SBF at 37 ± 1 °C.
Values of parameters for different coatings extracted from the EIS plot.

head.ce:title: Corrosion behavior and biological activity of micro-arc oxidation coating with puerarin on pure magnesium surface

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Heilongjiang Key Lab of Biomedical Materials, Jiamusi University, Jiamusi 154007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Heilongjiang Key Lab of Biomedical Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to improve the corrosion resistance and biological activity of micro-arc oxidation (MAO) coating on Mg alloys, a puerarin-loaded (PL) coating was prepared and applied on the MAO coating by the method of dip-coating in phytic acid (PA) solution with puerarin. The chemical and morphological characterizations of the coatings were examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). The corrosion properties of the coatings were studied by potentiodynamic polarization and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy in simulated body fluid (SBF). Besides, the bioactivity properties of the coatings in SBF were investigated following 3, 7, and 14 days of immersion. The results indicate that the defects of through-pores weaken the corrosion resistance of the MAO and PL coatings with a high cross-link density sealing of the pores of the MAO coating. The higher initial impedance, lower current density, and loss of weight show that PL coatings have higher corrosion resistance than do MAO coatings. Furthermore, PL coatings improve the bioactivity of the MAO coating by accelerating apatite mineralization. The use of such a coating is expected to promote the application of Mg in bone repair materials and improve bone healing rate in the early stages of implantation.

ce:section.ce:para: Magnesium and its alloys have attracted great attention in recent times due to their characteristics such as good biodegradability, degradation, and lower stress shielding in internal fixation materials [1,2]. Although Mg exhibits these advantages when used in implants, its poor corrosion properties limit its application in physiological environments in which it produces hydrogen gas and hydroxide ions [3–5]. Due to these disadvantages, different methods such as provision of protective films/coatings, surface modifications, and grain refinement, have been considered to improve its corrosion resistance [6,7]. Micro-arc oxidation (MAO) is an effective technique of surface modification employed in recent years to decrease the degradation rate of biodegradable Mg alloys [8,9]. It is also a preferred method in many fields because of its low cost and environmental advantages [10]. However, the micropores and microcracks generated in MAO become transportation channels easily, thereby increasing the opportunity for the aggressive medium to penetrate. Simultaneously, these defects provide mechanical interlocking sites, which can significantly improve the long-lasting adhesion of an organic or polymeric top coating applied to the substrate. Therefore, there have been some attempts to seal the porous MAO layer by sol-gel, addition of nano-particles in the electrolyte, and other treatments [11–16].

Phytic acid (PA), a non-toxic organic macro-molecule compound, is mainly extracted from cereal grains and seeds and has a strong metal chelating function with diverse metal ions such as Zn2+, Fe2+, Ca2+, and Mg2+ ions [17–19]. Among other applications, it is noteworthy that PA has been applied as an anti-corrosion coating, a complexometric reagent, and a cross-linking reagent [20]. Some authors have combined PA chemical pretreatment with other techniques such as electro-chemical deposition and sol-gel to improve the anti-corrosion performance of Mg alloys [21]. For e.g., Zhang et al. [22] reported that the corrosion resistance of MAO coatings can be improved by adding PA into an electrolyte of the coating. Zhang et al. [23] deposited a crack-free hydroxyapatite/PA (HA/PA) hybrid coating on an AZ31 Mg alloy substrate with a resultant increase in corrosion resistance.

Puerarin is a widely used Chinese medicine and is extracted from the traditional Chinese medicinal herb Radix Puerariae. A few studies have revealed that puerarin could promote osteoblast proliferation and differentiation to advance bone formation in vitro as well as induce additional new bone in bone defects [24–27]. Puerarin’s phenol hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the benzene ring both have the ability to coordinate metal ions [28].

The aim of this work is to prepare a puerarin-loaded (PL) composite functional coating to further improve the corrosion resistance and bioactivity of MAO coating. Therefore, we choose PA i.e., doped puerarin as a solution to seal the pores of the MAO coating. The morphological characteristics, chemical evolution, and electrochemical behavior of the coatings with puerarin of different concentrations were studied. In addition, we also investigated the bioactivity of PL coating in SBF.

Pure Mg in different shapes (thin samples: 10 mm × 10 mm × 1 mm, and 15 mm × 15 mm × 1 mm) was used in the experiments. The thin samples were sequentially polished to grits of 500, 1000, 1500 and 2000 by SiC papers. Next, the oxideand oil films on the samples were removed using NaHCO3 solution (80 g/L) and H3PO4 (100 g/L) solution prior to the process of MAO, and the samples were cleaned with ethanol. The following electrolyte was used in MAO:15 g/L Na2SiO3·9H1O, 10 g/L KOH, 8 g/L KF, and 1 g/L EDTA·2Na. The work voltage, work frequency, work duty cycle, and preparation time were 300 V, 500 Hz, 15%, and 10 min, respectively. The samples and stainless steel were used as the anode and cathode with an ultrasonic frequency of 60 kHz, respectively. Following MAO, the samples were washed with distilled water and dried in air. Subsequently, the samples were suspended by wires. They were then immersed in 3 mol/L NaOH solution at 60 °C for 1 h, which is referred to as alkali treatment. Then, they were washed with distilled water 3 times, and dried in a vacuum oven. The 7.5 g/L PA solution was prepared using ethanol as the solvent. Puerarin-loaded solutions in concentrations of 0.01 g/mL, 0.01 g/mL, and 0.03 g/mL were prepared using the 7.5 g/L PA solution as the solvent. The final coatings were obtained by immersion into PL solutions of different concentrations – 0 g/mL (i.e., PA solution), 0.01 g/mL, 0.02 g/mL, and 0.03 g/mL – at 40 °C for 100 s after MAO followed by a spin-dry, which was repeated thrice. The corresponding samples were named as PA, PL-0.01 g/mL, PL-0.02 g/mL, and PL-0.03 g/mL, respectively.

The microstructure and elemental composition of the coatings were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7800JJEOL) and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), respectively. The functional groups of the different coatings were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB250) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, Bruker VECTOR33) spectroscopy from 4000 cm−1 to 500 cm−1 at room temperature. Firstly, the coatings were scratched from the surface of samples and obtained powders were mixed with potassium bromide (KBr) in the weight ratio of 1:100, respectively. The mixed powders were then compacted under a hydrolytic pressure of 10 bar in order to form a transparent disc.

The XPS data were adjusted via the C1s base peak (284.8 eV). The phase analysis of the deposited films was conducted using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku D/max-2200/PC) with a grazing incidence of 1° at 40 kV and 36 mA using CuKα radiation.

The electrochemical behavior of the coatings in SBF was examined at 37 °C through potentiodynamic polarization tests and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) using an electrochemical workstation (VersaSTAT 3) at a scanning rate of 1 mV/s. The electrochemical equipment was a three-electrode cell system consisting of a glass test vessel, a platinum counter electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, and the working electrode (samples). The area exposed to the SBF was 1 cm2.

Each sample was placed in a polypropylene beaker with 20 mL of SBF and maintained at 37 ± 1 °C for 3, 7, and 14 days. After immersion, the samples were withdrawn from the SBF, gently washed with distilled water and then dried. The weight of the samples, before and after immersion in SBF, were measured using a 1/10,000 balance. The rate of weight loss is calculated using the following equation: R = (W1 − W0)/W0, in which W1 and W0 are defined as the weight of the samples before and after immersion in SBF, respectively.

The surface morphologies and corresponding EDS spectra of thee coatings are shown in Fig. 1 . The MAO coating has a typically porous morphology with many micropores, as shown in Fig. 1a. It has more surface pores than seen in the MAO coating treated with PA, which covers the pores in the latter, as shown in Fig. 1b. The surface morphologies of the PL coatings, which differ from the sample treated with PA alone, are shown in Fig. 1c, 1d, and 1e; these have few pores and are compact without microcracks all over the surface. The PL coatings exhibit different morphologies under different concentrations of puerarin. The surface of the PL-0.01 g/mL sample is covered with gunk (Fig. 1c) while the PL-0.02 g/mL sample has a snow-like morphology (Fig. 1d).

The main elements of the MAO coating samples are Mg, O, and Si, as the samples are prepared ina bath of silicate system (Fig. 1a). When the MAO coating is covered with PA, the elements C and P increase in content (Fig. 1b). The C content of the PA-puerarin coatings is significantly higher than that of the PA coating, while the Mg content decreases with the addition of puerarin (Fig. 1c–e). Table 1 summarizes the atomic contents for different coatings. When the MAO coating was covered with PA, the elements P increased in content. The C content of the PA-puerarin coatings was significantly higher than that of the PA coating, while the Mg content decreased with the addition of puerarin, which is consistent to EDS results, in Fig. 1. In addition, the C content reached 68.31% with the addition of puerarin as can be seen in Table 1.

Furthermore, Fig. 2 shows the cross-sections and elemental mapping of the MAO, PA, and PL-0.02 g/mL samples to confirm the defects of MAO coating, which can be seen from the through-pores (<1 μm) in the samples, and the pore coverage ability of the PL solution (Fig. 2b and c). The cross-sectional image of MAO coating (Fig. 2a) shows 7–8 μm thick defective columnar structure with discharge channels bonded to the Mg substrate. Accordingly, the corrosion resistance of the MAO coating is reduced by its defect. It can be noticed that the Mg, Si, and O elements are distributed uniformly throughout the cross-section of the coatings and the elemental mappings; this is because the coating is mainly composed of MgO and Mg2SiO4 in a silicate-based electrolyte [29]. In Fig. 2a and c, the presence of a P element band was observed on the cross-sections of the PA and PL-0.02 g/mL coatings due to the presence of P in the PA molecule, which indicates that regions I and II are PA coating and PL-0.02 g/mL coating, respectively. The thicknesses of the PA and PL coatings are approximately 1–2 μm (Fig. 2b and c) according to P element band of elemental mapping. The PL coating is more thick than PA coating with the addition of puerarin. Consequently, it is proved that the micropores of the MAO coating are sealed by the PA and PL-0.02 g/mL layers.

In order to confirm that the MAO surface can be covered with PA coating containing puerarin, XPS measurements were carried out to characterize the composition of the coatings, as shown in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3a illustrates the XPS survey spectrum and high-resolution XPS spectra of Mg 1s, O 1s, and Si 2p at different heights on the MAO coating. These spectral regions are shown in Fig. 3b–d. The binding energy of Mg 1s in the MAO coating is around 1303.69 eV, which correctly corresponds to the existence of Mg (1303.2 eV) and MgO (1303.9 eV) on the coating. The peak of O 1s at 536.1 eV can be assigned to the combination of Mg2SiO4 and MgO [30]. For Si 2p, the spectrum presented two Mg2SiO4 phases: 101.9 eV for α-Mg2SiO4, and 102.7 eV for γ-Mg2SiO4 [30]. The peak of P 2p appeared with a high absorption peak in the PL-0.02 g/mL coating due to the application of PA to the MAO coating in Fig. 3a. The spectral regions of C 1s, O 1s, and P 2p are shown in Fig. 3e–g for the PA coating. The C 1s spectrum in Fig. 3e has two discernable peaks: the C–C peak at 284.6 eV is due to the six-membered ring of the C element, and the other peak is due to the C–O bond in the PA. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3f) can be deconvoluted into five peaks at binding energies of 530.8 eV, 531.4 eV, 531.9 eV, 532.6 eV, and 533.2 eV. The peak located at the binding energy of 530.8 eV is attributed to the Mg–O bond in the MAO coating although the latter was covered with PA coating. The peak at 531.4 eV corresponds to the several OH− ions in PA molecules, and the spectrum is dominated by the OH− peak as expected based on the amount of OH− ions in a single PA molecule. Besides, the peaks at the binding energies of 531.9 eV and 532.6 eV correspond to P–O and C–O, respectively. The ratio of the P–O and C–O peak areas is close to 1:1, which isconsistent with the number of P–O and C–O bonds in PA molecules. The O 1s spectrum of the PA coating (Fig. 3f) displays a peak at around 533.2 eV, corresponding to the P–O–P bond produced by the dehydration of –OH in PA coating. The P 2p spectrum exhibits three peaks at 133.2 eV, 133.6 eV, and 134.2 eV (Fig. 3g). The peaks at around 133.2 eV and 134.2 eV correspond to P–OH and PO4 3− [31], respectively, and this is attributed to the six phosphates present in the PA molecules. The peak at 133.6 eV (Fig. 3g) is P–O–Mg [32], indicating that the PO4 3− of PA can form a chemical bond with Mg2+ in the MAO coating, producing a stable chemical conversion coating. However, the peaks of Mg 1s, P 2p, and Si 2p almost disappear while the peak intensity of C 1s and O 1s increases in the thicker PA-0.02 g/mL coating (Fig. 3a). The high-resolution spectra (Fig. 3h) of the C 1s region has two discernable spectra like that of the PA coating with many functional groups contributing to the peak envelope due to the complicated composition of puerarin. In Fig. 3h, the assignments include C–H (283.9 eV), phenolic group (284.5 eV), C–C (285.3 eV), C–O (286.0 eV), and CO (286.4 eV), which represent a few of the functional groups of puerarin. As seen in Fig. 3i, the O 1s spectrum is separated into four peaks: Mg–O (530.8 eV), –OH (531.4 eV), P–O (531.9 eV), and C–O (532.6 eV). However, the intensity of the binding energy of –OH decreases significantly, indicating that the –OH from PA and puerarin can lead to the formation of a hydrogen bond between them. Furthermore, the dominant C–O peak contributes towards the formation of the O 1s spectral region and is comparable to the dominant –OH peak of the PA coating.

Furthermore, the FTIR spectra were investigated to characterize the composition of the coatings and are displayed in Fig. 4 . The characteristic peaks at 518 cm−1 are assigned to the fundamental stretching vibration of Mg–O. The peaks at 1176 cm−1, 1053 cm−1, 1082 cm−1, 952 cm−1, and 854 cm−1 are due to the characteristic peaks of PA coating [33]. The band at 1082 cm−1 is connected with the PO4 3− group and the peak at 1053 cm−1 is primarily due to H2PO4 −1 group [34]. The peaks at around 941 cm−1 and 846 cm−1 are ascribed to the presence of P2O7 4−, and the enhanced peak at 1176 cm−1 is associated with the vibration of PO bonds. The incorporation of puerarin into PA coating is certified by the characteristic absorption peaks at 1120 cm−1 [35] and 1456 cm−1, which are due to the C–O–C stretching and benzene skeleton vibration stretching in puerarin. Besides, the absorption intensity decreased at 846 cm−1, 941 cm−1, 1082 cm−1, and 1176 cm−1 owing to the addition of puerarin into the PA coating, which is consistent with the P 2p peak of the XPS survey spectrum of the PL-0.02 g/mL coating sample.
Based on the effect of puerarin concentration on surface morphology, the suitable concentrations of puerarin were studied to obtain the best corrosion resistance of PL coating in SBF.

The polarization curves of the MAO, PA, and PL coatings are depicted in Fig. 5 . The corrosion potential (Ecorr) and corrosion current density (Icorr) derived from Fig. 5 are shown in Table 2 . The Ecorr and Icorr of the MAO coating are −1.77 V and 8.4 μA·cm−1, respectively. It is evident from Fig. 5 that all the PL coatings have more positive corrosion potentials and lower corrosion densities than the MAO coating. Besides, the concentrations of puerarin have an effect on Ecorr and Icorr in the case of PL coatings. The Icorr decreases from 1.658 μA·cm−1 to 1.589 μA·cm−1, 0.691 μA·cm−1, and 0.392 μA·cm−1 with increase in puerarin concentration from 0 g/mL (i.e., PA coating) to 0.03 g/mL. Therefore, the best corrosion resistance can be obtained by the addition of 0.03 g/mL of puerarin into the PA coating, which indicates that the corrosion resistance of the MAO coating is significantly enhanced with the application of PL coating.

To further understand the corrosion behavior of the PL coating system, AC impedance measurements were employed to evaluate the influence of puerarin additives on the corrosion resistance of the MAO coating. Fig. 6 shows the electrochemical impedance spectra of the MAO, PA, and PL coatings in SBF. The Nyquist plots of the MAO, PA, and PL coatings are shown in Fig. 6a. The diameter of the coatings increases gradually with puerarin concentration, which indicates that the modified coatings have higher corrosion resistance. Fig. 6b shows the Bode plots. The impedance modulus |Z| of the samples increases from 2.20 × 104 Ω·cm2 (MAO) to 6.35 × 104 Ω·cm2 (PA), 1.04 × 105 Ω·cm2 (PL-0.01 g/mL), 1.21 × 105 Ω·cm2 (PL-0.02 g/mL), and 1.31 × 105 Ω·cm2 (PL-0.03 g/mL), suggesting good corrosion protection conferred by the PL coating. Nevertheless, the different PL coatings exhibit similar |Z| values i.e., of the same magnitude. In Fig. 6c, the Bode phase plots of the different coatings are observed with two time constants on the high and medium frequency ranges due to the formation of the new interface between the MAO coating and PL coating, or the MAO coating and Mg substrate.

The equivalent circuit model in Fig. 6d is used to fit the EIS data of the MAO, PA and PL coatings, and the corresponding parameters of the different coatings are shown in Table 2. In Fig. 6d, Rs is the solution resistance, Rct is the charge transfer resistance, and R1 is the resistance of the PA and PL coatings. The capacitance of the PA or PL coatings and the constant phase element or the double layer capacitance are denoted by CPE1 and CPE2, respectively. In the case of the MAO coating, R1 and R2 represent the loose outer layer and dense inner layer, respectively, of the coating [36]. In general, a sample with higher Rct value and lower capacitance is more corrosion resistant [37]. As seen in Table 3 , the R1 values of all the PL coatings are higher than that of the MAO coating, while the R1 value of the PA coating is close to that of the MAO coating, which can be ascribed to the through-pores defect of the MAO coating and a thin monolayer of PA coating with a low cross-link density, respectively. However, the Rct value of the PA coating is higher than that of the MAO coating due to the sealing of the micropores of the latter. In addition, we found that the Rct values the of PL coatings are obviously higher than those of the MAO and PA layers, which indicates that the modified surfaces have higher capacities to inhibit chargetransfer. In addition, the Rct values of the PL coatings increase with puerarin concentration, which is mainly because puerarinserves asa thickener in the PA solution before dipping of the MAO coating. The results areconsistent withthose from the potentiodynamic polarization study. The above impedance analysis suggests that the corrosion performance of the MAO coating improves with PL treatment.

It is well know that Cl− significantly promotes corrosion of Mg due to transformation from Mg(OH)2 to MgCl2 when the PL coatings are immersed in body fluids. The cross-link density and networks of the coatings can weaken the penetration of Cl− into the Mg substrate [38]. The PA coating with puerarin has a high cross-link density and reinforced networks via the bonds between the PA and puerarin molecules, which can be identified by the high Rct values of the PL coatings. Therefore, the micro defects of the MAO coating can be sealed by PL coating with a cross-linked network structure to slow down the penetration of Cl− into the Mg substrate.

In order to establish the phase composition of the deposition products after immersion in SBF, the different coatings immersed for 7 days were studied by XRD analysis. The XRD patterns of the different coatings are shown in Fig. 9 . As shown in Fig. 9, MgSiO3 and HA are identified in the patterns on the surfaces of the different coatings. The peaks of HA at 32.9°, 43.85°, 48.67°, 63.0°, and 64.21° were observed on the MAO, PA and PL-0.02 g/mL coatings, suggesting that the degradation products of the three coatings contained the HA phase. Moreover, the peaks of HA at 31.79° and 32.16° were present in the case of the PL-0.02 g/mL coating, indicating that it can induce additional HA phase on the surface.

The PL coatings on MAO coating were prepared by dipping the latter in a PA solution with and without the addition of puerarin, and the morphology, composition, corrosion behavior, and bioactivity of the resultant coatings were studied. The following conclusions can be drawn:
The MAO coating has some through-pores of about 0.1–1 μm and these were sealed by the capillarity of the PL coatings to inhibit permeation of body fluid.
The corrosion resistance of the MAO coating was significantly improved by the coverage of PL coatings with higher impedance and lower current density. This is because the PL coating with high cross-link density and networks has a better capacity to inhibit the permeation of ions. In addition, the PL coating accelerated the formation of HA and showed excellent bioactivity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work described in this paper was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31370979).
